nativeRange.habitat: The Tiger Shovelnose Catfish inhabits large river channels, flooded plains, and river margins across major South American river systems, including the Amazon, Orinoco, and Paraná basins. In the water column, it ranges from mid-depth to the bottom, tending toward open-water zones rather than dense riparian vegetation.

decorPreferences: large smooth driftwood for cover and resting
minimal fine decor to prevent ingestion
large flat rocks as bottom substrate anchors
sparse planting — robust species only (will uproot plants)
open swimming space essential for active mid-column movement

diet.notes: The Tiger Shovelnose Catfish is an obligate piscivore in the wild, feeding on large fish, invertebrates, and crustaceans. In captivity it can generally be transitioned to large whole fish such as tilapia, smelt, or whitebait, along with raw prawns, mussels, and large carnivore pellets or sticks. Live feeder fish are not recommended because of the disease and nutritional imbalance risks they carry. Juveniles may accept large bloodworm and chopped fish flesh. Feeding every two to three days is sufficient for large adults. Overfeeding should be avoided, as uneaten protein degrades water quality rapidly.

goodWith: other large robust Amazonian fish of similar or larger size (e.g., large Arapaima in public-aquarium settings)
large Pacu (Colossoma macropomum) in very large public-aquarium displays
avoidWith: any fish smaller than its mouth width — will be eaten
conspecifics in confined space — territorial and cannibalistic at size

imageCandidates.notes: Bjoertvedt; 3548x1727. Sharp, well-lit complete lateral view clearly showing the diagnostic bold black vertical tiger bars over a pale grey body and silvery belly, shovel-shaped head and long barbels. Best diagnostic and technical match.

Matthieu Sontag (User:Mirgolth); 2340x812. Crisp lateral view of a different individual with strongly contrasting vertical tiger stripes and bright silvery-white belly; slight green cast / minor chromatic fringing but clearly diagnostic.

Vassil; 2647x1701. Striking three-quarter head-on portrait showing the broad flat shovel-shaped mouth and spread barbels with vertical banding on the flank; body/tail partly out of frame so kept as a non-recommended character shot.

summary: A massive Amazonian predator reaching 90–105 cm, patterned with bold black tiger stripes on a pale grey body. Sold as an ornamental juvenile but grows rapidly into a piscivore requiring public-aquarium scale facilities. Commercial food fish throughout South America; deeply unsuitable for home aquaria.
careNotes:
The Tiger Shovelnose Catfish is one of the largest members of the Pimelodidae family and one of the most frequently misrepresented species in the ornamental trade. Juveniles are sold at 5–12 cm and are striking in appearance, but the species grows rapidly — often reaching 40–50 cm within the first 12–18 months under good conditions — and ultimately attains 90–105 cm total length in the wild, with captive specimens regularly exceeding 80 cm. No home aquarium realistically houses an adult specimen; even a 3 m tank provides inadequate swimming room for a mature individual.

As a scaleless catfish (meaning the skin is largely unprotected by the bony plates, called scutes, found on many other catfish species), Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum is highly sensitive to copper. Copper-based medications, which are commonly used to treat ich and parasites, must be avoided entirely; alternatives such as heat treatment, aquarium salt, or formalin-based products are required instead. This sensitivity applies to both direct medication and incidental copper contamination from plumbing.

The species is an active piscivore and will consume any tankmate that fits within its broad, flat, shovel-shaped mouth. The mouth width often exceeds what keepers expect — fish approaching half the catfish’s own body length are at risk of predation. Escape risk is high; this species is a powerful jumper and can clear surprisingly low openings. A fully weighted, tightly fitted lid is essential.

The Tiger Shovelnose is a major commercial food fish throughout the Amazon basin and is farmed extensively in Brazil and Bolivia. Juvenile specimens are available from specialty and online retailers due to farming scale and low wholesale cost, which contributes to frequent sale to home aquarists who are not prepared for the adult size. The species is generally regarded as suitable only for public-aquarium facilities.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been achieved in home aquaria. Commercial aquaculture operations induce spawning through hormone injection — typically using carp pituitary extract — carried out in large outdoor raceways and ponds. In the wild, spawning is tied to the Amazon flood pulse: adults migrate into seasonally flooded forest areas, known as igapó, during high-water periods. Eggs are pelagic, meaning they drift freely in the water column rather than being deposited on a surface, and no parental care is provided. Home breeding is not feasible given the space requirements for adult fish and the need for hormonal induction.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 105 cm TL), temperature range (23–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Amazon, Orinoco, Paraná basins), diet (piscivore), scaleless copper-sensitivity note, and husbandry guidance on tank size requirements.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 105 cm TL, commonly to 90 cm), native range (Amazon and Paraná river basins across Brazil, Bolivia, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Paraguay, Argentina), taxonomy (Pimelodidae, Siluriformes), diet (carnivore/piscivore), commercial importance as food fish. Maximum weight recorded at 35 kg.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Tiger Shovelnose Catfish
additional.notes: Trade-hobbyist perspective. Confirmed adult size (over 3 feet / ~90 cm), piscivore diet, copper sensitivity as scaleless catfish, escape risk, minimum tank recommendation for juveniles (200+ gallons), and expert care classification. Emphasized species is not suitable for typical home aquariums long-term.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Giant catfish species guide
additional.notes: Referenced for hobbyist advisory context and confirmation of public-aquarium-only suitability. Noted that Pseudoplatystoma species are among the most common large Amazonian catfishes misrepresented in trade. Confirmed active piscivore nature and rapid growth rate.

additional.name: FishBase — Pseudoplatystoma fasciatum ecology and distribution
additional.notes: Ecology tab data: depth range, habitat (benthopelagic in large rivers and floodplains), migration patterns during Amazon flood pulse, aquaculture notes confirming hormone-induced commercial spawning. Tiger-banded coloration confirmed as species-typical pattern.

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. Commercial aquaculture uses hormone-induced spawning in large outdoor raceways; wild spawning is tied to Amazon flood-pulse seasonality. Captive home breeding is not feasible.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 90cm-1m+ in wild. Active piscivore. Sold tiny as ornamental but outgrows all home setups within months.
